title: Read a Pay Stub
summary: Read a pay stub and understand gross pay, deductions, and net pay.
learnerPromise: You will be able to look at a pay stub and understand where your money went before it reached you.
whyItMatters: Your pay stub explains how your paycheck was calculated and what was taken out, which helps you catch errors and plan your budget around the actual amount you receive.
realLifeUses: Checking that your hours and pay rate on a new job are correct
Understanding why your paycheck is smaller than your hourly rate times hours worked
Noticing if too much or too little tax was withheld
Using your year-to-date earnings to fill out a loan or rental application
materialsNeeded: A recent pay stub (paper or digital)
result: You can identify gross pay, each deduction, and net pay on any pay stub.
youWillLearn: Locate gross pay (total earnings before anything is taken out)
Identify common deductions like federal tax, state tax, and Social Security
Find optional deductions like health insurance or retirement contributions
Calculate net pay and understand year-to-date totals
miniChallenge: Look at a recent pay stub and list every deduction, then calculate what percentage of your gross pay you actually take home.
steps: Find a recent pay stub from a job, either printed or in an online portal.
Look at the top section for your name, pay period dates, and gross pay.
Read the deductions section and look for federal income tax, state tax, Social Security, and Medicare.
Look for any optional deductions like health insurance, retirement, or union dues.
Find net pay (take-home pay) and compare it to gross pay to see what portion you keep.
completionCriteria: You named at least four common deductions found on pay stubs
You calculated the difference between gross pay and net pay on a real or sample stub
commonProblems: Abbreviations like FIT, SIT, FICA, or YTD can be confusing at first
Different employers format pay stubs differently
Overtime pay or bonuses may appear on separate lines
tips: YTD means year-to-date and shows how much you have earned so far this year
If a deduction looks wrong, ask your payroll department or manager
Compare two consecutive pay stubs to spot unexpected changes
